ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Crab Rangoons. Closest match is the general wonton food product class; crab rangoon is a filled, deep-fried wonton variant.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for crab rangoon. A0FKP (filled pasta/dumplings, deep-fried) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for crab rangoon or equivalent filled fried wonton appetizer.

allergens: shellfish (potential — imitation crab may contain real crab or shellfish extract)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crab rangoons is approximately 90 g (about 4–5 pieces), as commonly served as an appetizer portion in Chinese-American restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Wontons, fried, with meat and/or seafood filling (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for crab rangoons as a named product. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for fried wontons with cream cheese and seafood filling, cross-referenced with USDA entries for cream cheese (FDC ID 173417) and imitation crab meat (FDC ID 175168). Saturated fat estimated at 9.5 g/100g based on cream cheese contribution (~40% of filling). Cholesterol estimated at 55 mg/100g. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available in USDA for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat-based fried dough products — analogous estimate based on fried wheat flour wonton wrappers
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crab rangoons in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 (high) is estimated by analogy with deep-fried wheat flour dough products (GI ~70–75) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The refined wheat flour wonton wrappers are the primary glycemic driver. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 19.35) / 100 ≈ 13.5 (carbohydrate per 90g serving = 21.5 × 0.9 = 19.35 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg/100g) and saturated fat from cream cheese filling
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit crab rangoon consumption due to the high sodium load (405 mg per 90g serving) and saturated fat content, both of which can elevate blood pressure and increase cardiovascular strain; portion control and avoidance of accompanying high-sodium dipping sauces is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) from refined wheat flour wonton wrappers and moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat crab rangoons as an occasional indulgence only, as the high-GI refined wheat wrappers and a glycemic load of approximately 13.5 per serving can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with low-GI foods and limiting portion to 2–3 pieces may help moderate glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) from cream cheese and deep-frying oil, combined with elevated sodium and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit crab rangoons, as the combination of high saturated fat (~8.6 g per 90g serving), elevated sodium, and deep-frying process contributes to atherogenic risk and may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential from deep-frying and refined carbohydrates
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption of deep-fried, refined-carbohydrate foods such as crab rangoons, as regular intake of such foods is associated with increased systemic inflammation that may exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab Rangoons are a deep-fried appetizer originating in mid-20th century American Chinese (Tiki-style Polynesian) restaurant culture, now a staple of Chinese-American dining. Each piece consists of a thin, crisp wonton wrapper made from refined wheat flour, folded into a star or triangle shape and filled with a savory mixture of cream cheese, flaked crab meat or imitation crab (surimi), scallions, and garlic. The filling is rich, hot, and creamy, contrasting with the light, crunchy, golden-brown exterior. They are typically served in portions of 4–6 pieces (~90g) alongside sweet and sour sauce or hot mustard. Nutritionally, crab rangoons are energy-dense at approximately 310 kcal per 100g, with a high fat content (~22.5g/100g, predominantly from cream cheese and frying oil), moderate carbohydrates (~21.5g/100g from the wonton wrapper), and moderate protein (~6.5g/100g from crab/surimi and cream cheese). Sodium is elevated at 450 mg/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high), driven by the refined wheat flour wrapper. Clinically, crab rangoons are unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to their high sodium, saturated fat, and high-GI carbohydrate profile. They are best consumed as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary component.

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for crab rangoons as a named composite product. Nutritional values are adapted from USDA data for fried wontons with cream cheese and seafood filling components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on fried wheat flour dough products. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this composite deep-fried cream cheese and crab wonton preparation are not available as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved primary source as of 2024. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100g) and cholesterol (55 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA component data for cream cheese (FDC ID 173417) and imitation crab (FDC ID 175168) proportioned to a typical filling ratio. The input record listed two identical portion entries ('1 serving (90g)'); the duplicate has been collapsed to a single canonical serving of 90g. The input dietary_tag 'low_sugar' has been removed as it is inconsistent with the food's overall nutritional profile (high fat, high sodium, high GI); sugar content of 2.5g/100g is low but the food is not clinically categorized as low-sugar in context. Vitamin A value of 120 mcg RAE/100g is carried from input and is plausible given cream cheese contribution. All core macros (protein_g 6.5, fat_g 22.5, carbohydrate_g 21.5) are non-zero and sourced from USDA FoodData Central adapted composite data.
